body_txt: A practitioner of a specific kind of Whatevermancy , Necromancers are the masters of the art of death. Necromancy, the magic of Interrogating the Dead , has been practiced since at least Ancient Greece , where it was known as Necromantia , and probably earlier. In typical fantasy media they're the epitome of the Evil Sorcerer , showing none of the respect in Due to the Dead , using The Dark Arts to create an army of skeletons , zombies , undead animals and undead monsters (or any kind of Undead being) to ravage the countryside or enslave souls of the damned for their own evil and twisted purposes, with the most ambitious examples being a horrifying combination of Evil Overlord and Omnicidal Maniac . When they don't have undead servants to protect themselves, their offensive magic will make use of villainous elements that are thematically related to death, such as decay , disease , poison , shadow , and/or spirit energy . If they lead a faction, it will be made up of enthralled corpses , to whom the Necromancer acts as a Hive Queen . Any villain that practices necromancy is virtually guaranteed to fall hard onto the far side of the evil scale, and any hero who dares to dabble in it is likely to be seen as having fallen into evil by others. As a general rule, this is an offshoot of the aversion that most cultures have for interfering with the dead — Grave Robbing , Desecrating the Dead , and dissection of human bodies have historically been considered wrong and immoral over most of history due to being seen as desecrating the rest of the departed, tampering with spiritually unclean and "polluting" material, or both. Thus, the archetype of the unnatural magician who uses evil magic to tamper with the dead is essentially a magnified version of the "evil graverobber" character type. It's not unheard of for a necromancer to be one of The Undead themselves, often a lich . Even if they aren't liches or other forms of undead themselves, they are likely to have unlocked other ways of prolonging their own lives to unnatural lengths. Furthermore, they may become partially undead . While generally villains, necromancers ocassionally appear as protagonists, mostly in role-playing games and works in an RPG Mechanics 'Verse . These necromancers tend to use their powers to commune with the dead , heal others , or use their power over souls for good ends. Whereas villainous necromancers are essentially "enlarged" versions of depictions of graverobbers, these tend to have more to do with historic portrayals of medius, shamans, and other figures viewed as speaking with the spirits of the departed. But since that's not nearly as interesting as zombies , the idea of a necromancer unleashing waves of zombies still persists. Of course , that doesn't stop them from unleashing the hordes on the bad guys instead! . Commonly overlaps with Necromantic ; either someone attempts necromancy in order to bring back their loved one, or this was the start of their descent into evil . Sometimes a character will approach a necromancer for their help in bringing back their beloved (which never ends well). Expect An Aesop about how you should respect the cycle of life and death and " Be Careful What You Wish For ." Since necromancers are typically seen animating not only still-fleshy corpses as zombies but also completely skeletal remains, Fridge Logic may raise the question of why there exists an arbitrary point past which a body is too decomposed to reanimate. After all, if the necromancer's magic can both hold together the bones of a skeleton despite the absence of ligaments and provide it with motor functions despite the absence of muscles, why then does the skeleton suddenly cease to function when those same bones get pulverized by the knight's warhammer? Following this, one might wonder if a necromancer could animate the ashes from an urn, or that hamburger you ate yesterday currently inside your small intestine . In some settings, necromancers indeed can keep "reviving" defeated undead thanks to the Undead Resurrection Clause , though likely there are some diminishing returns in terms of physical integrity or Hit Points . In video games, Necromancer enemies will generally lead a Keystone Army where they also Revive defeated undead, and the Gemini Destruction Law means you need to defeat the army to get to the necromancer to permanently defeat said army. Almost always clad in a Black Cloak , Skeletons in the Coat Closet , spooky Skeleton Motifs , a Sinister Deer Skull helmet, or some combination thereof. Very commonly sporting White Hair, Black Heart (or Mystical White Hair , if benevolent). See also Animate Dead , Tested on Humans and The Necrocracy . If the necromancer is juxtaposed with a healing or nature-controlling character, see Life/Death Juxtaposition . See also Fossil Revival . Only metaphorically related to Thread Necromancer , where one resurrects dead forum threads.
